content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 25, 1991

Diedre' D. Lingenfelter, CMC
City Clerk
City of Corona
815 West Sixth Street
P.O. Box 940
Corona, CA 91718

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
    Our File No. I-91-262

Dear Ms. Lingenfelter:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Corona City Councilmember Bill Franklin regarding his
responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since your advice request does
not refer to a specific governmental decision but instead seeks
general guidance, we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance.²

QUESTION

     May Councilmember Franklin participate in city council
decisions which may materially affect clients of his employer on
whose projects the councilmember has not been involved?

CONCLUSION

     Councilmember Franklin is required to disqualify himself from
any decision before the city council which would foreseeably and
materially affect any economic interest, including his employer

___________________________
1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2  Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-91-262
Page 2


and clients on whose accounts he has worked, if the effect on the
economic interest would be different from the effect on the public
generally.

FACTS

     Councilmember Franklin is employed as a loan officer at a
mortgage company located in Riverside. The company works with
property throughout the western region of Riverside County. For
each sales transaction on the councilmember works on, the
councilmember and the broker receive a commission. He receives no
commission for property transactions in which he does not
participate.

     Councilmember Franklin has become concerned that he has a
conflict of interest with respect to any city council decision
concerning any client of his employer. You stated that the
councilmember has no access to information concerning transactions
in which he is not involved.

ANALYSIS

     Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. A "public official" is
defined in Section 82048 and Regulation 18700 (copy enclosed) as
every natural person who is a member, officer, employee, or
consultant of a state or local government agency. This definition
would include a member of the Corona City Council.

     Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

     (c) Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institu-
tion in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made.

     (d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

Section 87103(c) and (d).

     Councilmember Franklin is a loan officer for a mortgage
company that does business in the jurisdiction. As an employee of

Our File No. I-91-262
Page 3


the company, the councilmember has an economic interest in the
company. Moreover, in the course of his work, the councilmember
will receive commission income for each real estate transaction in
which he participates.

Generally, commission income is treated as income derived
from all the participants in the transaction. Further, Regulation
18704.3 (copy enclosed) provides:

     (a)  This section contains the disclosure and
disqualification requirements for any public
official who receives commission income for
services rendered as an insurance broker or agent,
a real estate broker or agent, a travel agent or
salesperson, a stockbroker or a retail or wholesale
salesperson.

     (b)  "Commission income" means gross payments
received as a result of services rendered as a
broker, agent, or other salesperson for a specific
sale or similar transaction. Commission income is
received when it is paid or credited.

     (c)  The sources of commission income in a
specific sale or similar transaction include for
each of the following:

* * *

     (2)  A real estate broker:

          (A)  The person the broker
represents in the transaction;

          (B)  If the broker receives a
commission from a transaction conducted
by an agent working under the broker's
auspices, the person represented by the
agent;

          (C)  Any brokerage business entity
through which the broker conducts
business; and

          (D)  Any person who receives a
finder's or other referral fee for
referring a party to the transaction to
the broker, or who makes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker.

* * *

     (d)  For purposes of determining whether
disqualification is required under the provisions

Our File No. I-91-262
Page 4


of Sections 87100 and 87103(c), the full gross
value of any commission income for a specific sale
or similar transaction shall be attributed to each
source of income in that sale or transaction.

While not directly applicable, we can apply Regulation
18704.3 by analogy. According to your facts the councilmember
receives commission income for each real estate transaction he
participates in as a loan officer. Thus, according to the
regulation, each commission the councilmember receives should be
attributed in full to both the councilmember's employer and the
client involved in the transactions for which the councilmember
received a commission. Other clients of the mortgage company
would not be economic interests of the councilmember.

Thus, Councilmember Franklin is required to disqualify
himself from any decision before the city council which would
foreseeably and materially affect any economic interest, including
his employer and clients on whose accounts he has worked, if the
effect on the economic interest would be different from the effect
on the public generally. (Section 87103(c).)

Furthermore, these persons will remain economic interests of
Councilmember Franklin for purposes of the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act for 12 months after the commission income
was received by the councilmember. (Section 87103(c).)
Therefore, for a period up to one year, Councilmember Franklin
also will be required to disqualify himself from participating in
any city council decision which will have a foreseeable and
material financial effect on his employer or clients on whose
accounts he has worked.

Foreseeable Material Financial Effects on Economic Interests

Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reason-
ably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198,
copy enclosed.)

In addition, the effect on the councilmember's economic
interest must be material in order to require disqualification.
The Commission has adopted differing guidelines to determine
whether an effect is material, depending on the specific
circumstances of each decision. For example, where the mortgage

Our File No. I-91-262
Page 5


company is directly before the city council,3 as an applicant or
the subject of the decision, Regulation 18702.1(a) (copy enclosed)
provides that the effect of the decision on the mortgage company
is deemed material and disqualification is required. (Combs
Advice Letter, No. A-89-177, copy enclosed.)

     Where the economic interest is not directly before the city
council, but may be indirectly affected, Regulations 18702.2 and
18702.6 (copies enclosed) apply.  Whether the indirect effect on a
business entity, such as the councilmember's employer is material
depends on the financial size of the business entity.  For
example, Regulation 18702.2(g) provides that for a relatively
small business entity, the indirect effect of a decision is
material where:

          (1)  The decision will result in an increase
     or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
     of $10,000 or more; or

          (2)  The decision will result in the business
     entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
     reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
     fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

          (3)  The decision will result in the increase
     or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
     of $10,000 or more.

     Moreover, where the economic interest is a person and not
directly before the city council, such as a client for which the
city councilmember has worked, Regulation 18702.6 provides:

          The effect of a decision is material as to an
     individual who is a source of income or gifts to an
     official if any of the following applies:

               (a)  The decision will affect the
          individual's income, investments, or other
          tangible or intangible assets or liabilities
          (other than real property) by $1,000 or more;
          or


_______________
3    The mortgage company is considered directly before the city
council when the company initiates the proceeding by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request, or is named
party in, or the subject of, the proceeding.  A person or business
entity is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person or business entity. (Regulation 18702.1(b).)

Our File No. I-91-262
Page 6


          (b)  The decision will affect the
     individual's real property interest in a man-
     ner that is considered material under Section
     18702.3 or Section 18702.4.

     Where a financial effect on an economic interest will be both
foreseeable and material, the councilmember may not participate.

Other Clients of the Mortgage Company

     With respect to any clients of the mortgage company for whom
the councilmember did not provide services, Councilmember Franklin
may participate in decisions concerning such clients, provided the
decision will not have a material financial effect on the mortgage
company. (See, Regulation 18702.2.) Thus, the effect of a
decision on other clients of the mortgage company should only be
considered to the extent that the effect on that client will
result in an increase or decrease in the gross revenues, the value
of the assets or liabilities, or expenses of the mortgage company.
Effects on the other clients of the mortgage company which will
not affect the mortgage company itself do not result in a conflict
of interest.

Public Generally Exception

     Finally, even if Councilmember Franklin determines that he
has a financial interest that will be materially affected by the
decision, he may still participate in the decision if the effect
on the councilmember's financial interest is not distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally. For the "public
generally" exception to apply, the decision must affect the source
of income in substantially the same manner as it will affect a
significant segment of Corona. (Regulation 18703, copy enclosed.)
You have not provided facts concerning a specific decision so we
can only provide the general guidelines set forth above.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        By:  John W. Wallace
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JW:aa

Enclosures
